g.text: Hadoop MapReduce for Batch Data Analytics

g.data-narration: MapReduce is a programming model for processing large datasets across distributed clusters, enabling parallel processing with automatic fault tolerance.
g.text: A programming model for parallel processing of large datasets in distributed environments.
Breaks processing into Map (transform) → Shuffle/Sort (organize) → Reduce (aggregate) phases.

g.data-narration: MapReduce follows five key principles: divide and conquer, data locality, parallel processing, fault tolerance, and linear scalability.
The Map phase processes input records and produces intermediate key-value pairs. Each mapper processes data independently and in parallel.
In Word Count example, map function splits lines into words and emits (word, 1) for each occurrence.
Shuffle and Sort transfers mapper outputs to reducers, groups values by key, and maintains sorted order for efficient aggregation.
A combiner is a local reducer that runs on mapper nodes to reduce network transfer by aggregating values before shuffling.
g.text: Reduces network traffic by combining mapper outputs locally. Only works for associative operations like sum, count, max.

g.data-narration: The Reduce phase aggregates grouped values to produce final output. Each reducer processes all values for its assigned keys.
The complete MapReduce workflow goes through job submission, map task execution, shuffle/sort, reduce execution, and job completion.
In the complete Word Count example, mappers emit word counts, shuffle groups them, and reducers sum to produce final frequencies.
MapReduce enables IoT batch analytics including sensor data aggregation, device log analysis, energy monitoring, and predictive maintenance.
Key optimizations include combiners for local aggregation, compression for reduced I/O, and proper reducer count for balanced workloads.
g.text: Schedule tasks on nodes with data

g.data-narration: MapReduce offers simplicity, scalability, and fault tolerance but has high latency and is inefficient for iterative processing.
g.text: Simplicity • Scalability • Fault Tolerance • Data Locality • Cost-Effective
High Latency • Disk I/O Overhead • Not for Real-time • Iterative Processing Inefficient

g.data-narration: Remember: Map transforms data, Shuffle groups by key, Reduce aggregates. Word Count is the classic example. Use combiners and data locality for optimization.
g.text: Word Count: Classic Example • Combiner: Local Aggregation • Data Locality: 10-20x Faster
